items.foco: Classification of acute and chronic gastritis
items.title: Classification of acute and chronic gastritis
items.contentHtml:
Classification of acute and chronic gastritis

  • Acute gastritis is classified as erosive (most common due to non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs or stress) and non-erosive (infectious, due to initial Helicobacter pylori, or due to radiation).
  • Chronic gastritis is histologically divided into atrophic (intestinal metaplasia, risk of adenocarcinoma). gastric) and non-atrophic (superficial, usually due to Helicobacter pylori).
  • Sydney classification: systematizes endoscopic and histological findings, evaluating topography (antrum, body, pangastritis), morphology (erythema, erosions, folds) and etiology.
  • Key differential diagnosis: functional dyspepsia does not present histological or endoscopic changes significant.

items.title: Most common cause: Helicobacter pylori
items.contentHtml:
Most common cause: Helicobacter pylori

  • Helicobacter pylori is the most common cause of chronic gastritis worldwide, infecting approximately 50% of the population.
  • Mechanism: bacterial urease hydrolyzes urea into ammonium, neutralizing the gastric acid pH and allowing colonization of the mucosa.
  • Chronic infection leads to chronic active gastritis, with neutrophilic and lymphocytic infiltrate, which can progress to atrophic gastritis and intestinal metaplasia.
  • Non-invasive test of choice for initial diagnosis: carbon-13 urea breath test (sensitivity >95%).

items.title: Diagnosis: endoscopy with biopsy
items.contentHtml:
Diagnosis: endoscopy with biopsy

  • The gold standard for the diagnosis of gastritis is upper digestive endoscopy with gastric biopsy, which allows histological evaluation and culture.
  • It is recommended to take at least 2 biopsies from the antrum and 2 from the body (Sydney protocol) to classify the type and extent of gastritis.
  • Finding key endoscopic examination in chronic gastritis due to Helicobacter pylori: antral nodular erythema (“cobblestone” pattern).
  • The biopsy allows Giemsa or Warthin-Starry staining to identify Helicobacter pylori, and evaluate atrophy, intestinal metaplasia or dysplasia.

items.foco: Triple therapy treatment for H. pylori
items.title: Triple therapy treatment for H. pylori
items.contentHtml:
Triple therapy treatment for H. pylori

  • The first line of treatment in areas with low resistance to clarithromycin (<15%) is triple therapy for 14 days: proton pump inhibitor (omeprazole 20 mg every 12 hours), amoxicillin 1 g every 12 hours and clarithromycin 500 mg every 12 hours.
  • In those allergic to penicillin, amoxicillin is replaced by metronidazole 500 mg every 12 hours (triple therapy with metronidazole).
  • If resistance to clarithromycin is high (>15%), quadruple therapy with bismuth is preferred: inhibitor proton pump, bismuth subsalicylate 524 mg every 6 hours, metronidazole and tetracycline.
  • Verify eradication with urea breath test or fecal antigen at least 4 weeks after finishing treatment.

items.title: Erosive gastritis due to NSAIDs
items.contentHtml:
Erosive gastritis due to NSAIDs

  • Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) inhibit cyclooxygenase-1, reducing the synthesis of protective prostaglandins in the gastric mucosa, causing acute or chronic erosive gastritis.
  • Risk factors: age >60 years, high doses, concomitant use of anticoagulants or corticosteroids, history of peptic ulcer.
  • Presentation: multiple erosions, petechiae or ulcers in the antrum and gastric body; may bleed (melena, hematemesis).
  • Management: discontinue the non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug if possible, and start proton pump inhibitor (omeprazole 20-40 mg/day) for 4-8 weeks; For prophylaxis, proton pump inhibitor or misoprostol is used.

items.foco: Chronic atrophic gastritis and cancer risk
items.title: Chronic atrophic gastritis and cancer risk
items.contentHtml:
Chronic atrophic gastritis and risk of cancer

  • Chronic atrophic gastritis is characterized by loss of gastric glands, with intestinal metaplasia (type I, II or III), and is associated with a higher risk of gastric adenocarcinoma of the intestinal type (Correa sequence).
  • Autoimmune atrophic gastritis affects mainly to the body/fundus, with achlorhydria, hypergastrinemia and anti-parietal cell and anti-intrinsic factor antibodies.
  • Risk of gastric cancer: 2-6 times higher in atrophic gastritis; Endoscopic surveillance is recommended every 1-3 years with biopsies.
  • Complete intestinal metaplasia (type I) has a lower risk than incomplete metaplasia (type III, sulfomucin positive).

items.title: Menetrier's disease: giant folds
items.contentHtml:
Menetrier's disease: giant folds

  • Menetrier's disease is a rare hypertrophic gastropathy, characterized by giant gastric folds in the body/fundus, with hyperplastic fovea and glandular atrophy.
  • Clinical: hypoproteinemia due to protein loss (exudative gastropathy), edema, epigastric pain, nausea and diarrhea.
  • Associated with cytomegalovirus infection in children and overexpression of epidermal growth factor (TGF-alpha).
  • Treatment: nutritional support, proton pump inhibitors, and in severe cases, subtotal gastrectomy; The risk of gastric adenocarcinoma is low but exists.

items.title: Autoimmune gastritis and pernicious anemia
items.contentHtml:
Autoimmune gastritis and pernicious anemia

  • Autoimmune gastritis is a T lymphocyte-mediated disease that destroys the parietal cells of the body/fundus, causing achlorhydria and intrinsic factor deficiency.
  • Intrinsic factor deficiency leads to pernicious anemia (megaloblastic anemia due to vitamin deficiency B12), with anti-intrinsic factor antibodies in 50-70% of cases.
  • Findings: hypergastrinemia (due to loss of acid feedback), intestinal metaplasia, risk of gastric neuroendocrine tumors (carcinoids) type I.
  • Treatment: intramuscular vitamin B12 replacement for life (cyanocobalamin 1000 µg monthly) and endoscopic surveillance every 2-3 years.

items.foco: Urea breath test for H. pylori
items.title: Urea breath test for H. pylori
items.contentHtml:
Urea breath test for H. pylori

  • The carbon-13 labeled urea breath test is a non-invasive test of choice for initial diagnosis and confirmation of Helicobacter pylori eradication.
  • Rationale: Helicobacter pylori urease hydrolyzes the labeled urea, releasing carbon-13 dioxide that is detected in the breath.
  • Sensitivity and specificity >95%; requires fasting for 4-6 hours and suspending proton pump inhibitors 2 weeks before, and antibiotics 4 weeks before.
  • It should not be used if the patient has had gastric surgery (false negatives due to transit alteration).

items.title: Complication: peptic ulcer and bleeding
items.contentHtml:
Complication: peptic ulcer and bleeding

  • The peptic ulcer is the most frequent complication of chronic gastritis due to Helicobacter pylori or non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, with the risk of upper digestive bleeding.
  • Clinic: melena, hematemesis, epigastric pain; active bleeding is classified by the Forrest scale: Forrest Ia (active jet bleeding), Forrest Ib (active sheet bleeding), Forrest IIa (visible non-bleeding vessel).
  • Acute management: resuscitation, therapeutic endoscopy (adrenaline injection, coagulation, clip), and bolus intravenous proton pump inhibitor (omeprazole). 80 mg, then infusion 8 mg/hour for 72 hours).
  • The mortality of bleeding from peptic ulcer is 5-10%.

items.foco: Stress gastritis in critically ill patients
items.title: Stress gastritis in critically ill patients
items.contentHtml:
Stress gastritis in critically ill patients

  • Stress gastritis occurs in critically ill patients (intensive care unit) due to splanchnic hypoperfusion, mucosal ischemia and reperfusion, with acute erosions in the gastric body.
  • Risk factors: mechanical ventilation >48 hours, coagulopathy (platelet count <50,000/µL or INR >1.5), kidney failure, extensive burns (Curling's ulcer), head trauma (Cushing's ulcer).
  • Prophylaxis: in patients with at least two risk factors, proton pump inhibitor (omeprazole 40 mg/day intravenous) or H2 antagonist (ranitidine) is recommended.
  • The endoscopy shows multiple erosions and petechiae; Bleeding can be massive, but the incidence has decreased with routine prophylaxis.

items.title: Differential diagnosis with functional dyspepsia
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis with functional dyspepsia

  • Functional dyspepsia is defined by symptoms of epigastric pain, burning, postprandial fullness or early satiety, without evidence of organic disease on endoscopy (Rome IV criteria).
  • Key differentiation: in gastritis, endoscopy with biopsy shows histological inflammation; in functional dyspepsia, the mucosa is normal or with minimal non-specific changes.
  • The urea breath test allows us to rule out Helicobacter pylori infection as an organic cause; If it is positive, it is treated and re-evaluated.
  • Empiric treatment with proton pump inhibitors without endoscopy is not recommended in patients >60 years of age or with warning signs (weight loss, anemia, dysphagia, palpable mass).
